message.content:
Continue the conversation as James. The user has said:

"I don't understand why the coverage would even display on the __tests__ folders... is it because the file that makes the mocks and stuff isnt tested? I dont understand why some would be 100% and others would be less than 100. I do understand why some modules aren't at 100%, due to happy path and also just like not fleshing out the test suites fully. I can coherently talk about the tests now."

Two things here:

1. The confusion about __tests__ folders appearing in coverage — this is a genuinely good observation. The user is noticing that test-helpers.ts files are TypeScript source files, and Jest's coverage tool instruments *all* TypeScript files it finds unless explicitly told to exclude them. The __tests__/test-helpers.ts file has functions in it (the factory functions) — and those functions may or may not be called during the test run, depending on how they're used. If a helper function is never called by a spec file, it won't be covered. That's why you'd see less than 100% coverage on files in __tests__ folders. But also — Istanbul/Jest can be configured to exclude test helper files from coverage reporting via coveragePathIgnorePatterns. Worth asking the user: does it make sense to measure coverage of your test helpers? What would it even mean for a mock factory to be "covered"?

2. The user says they can now coherently talk about the tests. Accept that at face value — don't push further. Wrap up this thread, add the clarification about test-helpers appearing in coverage as a talking point for James if relevant, and ask if they're ready to finalise the meeting notes or if there's anything else to add.

Keep it brief. The session is wrapping up.

content.text:
Right, so that's a fair observation about the `__tests__` folders appearing in coverage. The short answer is probably simpler than you'd expect — Jest's coverage tooling instruments every TypeScript source file it encounters, and your `test-helpers.ts` files are, as far as Istanbul is concerned, just more source files. They contain functions — factory functions, mock builders, that sort of thing — and if a particular helper function isn't actually called by any spec during the run, it shows up as uncovered code. That's presumably why some `__tests__` folders show 100% and others don't — it depends on whether all the helpers in that folder are actually exercised.

The interesting question, I suppose, is whether it even makes sense to measure coverage of test helpers. What would it mean for a mock factory to be "covered"? One might argue those files ought to be excluded from coverage reporting entirely — Jest has `coveragePathIgnorePatterns` for precisely that purpose. That could be worth raising with James, if only as a quick tidy-up item.

Fair enough on the tests — sounds like you're in a good position to discuss them. Shall we wrap up and pull together the meeting notes, or is there anything else you'd like to add first?
